vocabulary.example_sentence: Classical conditioning explains why certain smells trigger strong memories.

vocabulary.definition_ar: تحيز معرفي يؤثر على القرارات والأحكام بشكل منهجي
vocabulary.example_sentence: Her cognitive bias made her interpret neutral comments as personal attacks.

vocabulary.definition_ar: قهري، إجباري
vocabulary.example_sentence: His compulsive checking of doors made him late for work.

vocabulary.definition_ar: إزالة الحساسية، تقليل الاستجابة
vocabulary.example_sentence: Systematic desensitization can help people overcome animal phobias.

vocabulary.definition_ar: دفاعي، متحفظ
vocabulary.example_sentence: He became defensive when his friends questioned his decision.

vocabulary.definition_ar: حالة من الألم الشديد أو المعاناة أو القلق المفرط.
vocabulary.example_sentence: The lost child was in great distress until she found her mother.

vocabulary.definition_ar: القدرة على إدارة التجارب العاطفية والتعامل معها بفاعلية
vocabulary.example_sentence: Learning emotional regulation is key to good mental health.

vocabulary.definition_ar: فقدان تام للأمل في موقف صعب.
vocabulary.example_sentence: After failing many times, he almost gave in to despair.

vocabulary.definition_ar: يتصاعد، يتفاقم
vocabulary.example_sentence: Small arguments can escalate into major relationship problems.

vocabulary.definition_ar: حالة يشعر فيها الشخص بمشاعر ضئيلة أو معدومة، غالباً بعد صدمة
vocabulary.example_sentence: Emotional numbness can be the mind's way of protecting itself.

vocabulary.definition_ar: القدرة على فهم ومشاركة مشاعر شخص آخر
vocabulary.example_sentence: Good therapists show empathy while maintaining professional boundaries.

vocabulary.definition_ar: الاستجابة التلقائية للجسم للاستعداد للخطر بالقتال أو الفرار
vocabulary.example_sentence: The fight-or-flight response makes your heart beat faster.

vocabulary.definition_ar: حالة من اليقظة الشديدة تجاه التهديدات أو المخاطر المحتملة
vocabulary.example_sentence: Hypervigilance after the accident made it hard for her to sleep.

vocabulary.definition_ar: علاج نفسي يواجه فيه المرضى مخاوفهم في بيئة آمنة
vocabulary.example_sentence: Exposure therapy is one of the most effective treatments for phobias.

vocabulary.definition_ar: يستوعب، يدخل في نفسيته
vocabulary.example_sentence: Children often internalize their parents' attitudes about success and failure.

vocabulary.definition_ar: مرتفع، مكثف، محتد
vocabulary.example_sentence: During emergencies, people experience heightened awareness of their surroundings.

vocabulary.definition_ar: شديد اليقظة، متأهب بشكل مفرط
vocabulary.example_sentence: After the robbery, she became hypervigilant about home security.

vocabulary.definition_ar: فكرة غير مرغوب فيها ومزعجة تتسلل إلى الذهن فجأة
vocabulary.example_sentence: Intrusive thoughts are common but do not reflect a person's true wishes.

vocabulary.definition_ar: ينفجر، يهاجم بعنف
vocabulary.example_sentence: When stressed, some people lash out at those closest to them.

vocabulary.definition_ar: تطفلي، مقتحم للخصوصية
vocabulary.example_sentence: Intrusive thoughts about accidents made it hard for her to drive.

vocabulary.definition_ar: غير منطقي، غير عقلاني
vocabulary.example_sentence: She knows her fear of elevators is irrational but can't control it.

vocabulary.definition_ar: مخدر للمشاعر، مُبلد
vocabulary.example_sentence: The numbing effect of grief made it hard to enjoy anything.

vocabulary.definition_ar: إسقاط، نسب المشاعر للآخرين
vocabulary.example_sentence: His anger might be projection of his own fears onto others.

vocabulary.definition_ar: شعور بحزن عميق يستمر لفترة طويلة.
vocabulary.example_sentence: The rainy weather gave him a sense of melancholy.

vocabulary.definition_ar: ينظم، يتحكم في
vocabulary.example_sentence: Deep breathing exercises help regulate emotions during stressful moments.

vocabulary.definition_ar: القلق المستمر والصعوبة العاطفية بعد تجربة حدث صادم
vocabulary.example_sentence: Post-traumatic stress affects soldiers returning from combat zones.

vocabulary.definition_ar: العملية التي تُشكّل فيها التجارب المتكررة الاستجابات العاطفية المستقبلية
vocabulary.example_sentence: Psychological conditioning explains why some fears develop in childhood.

vocabulary.definition_ar: شعور بالهدوء والسكينة والتحرر من الضغوط.
vocabulary.example_sentence: She found serenity by sitting quietly near the lake.

vocabulary.definition_ar: ينفجر فجأة، يفقد أعصابه
vocabulary.example_sentence: After weeks of pressure, he finally snapped at his colleague.

vocabulary.definition_ar: الثقة في قيمة الذات أو القدرات؛ احترام الذات
vocabulary.example_sentence: Building self-esteem is crucial for overcoming social anxiety and fear.

vocabulary.definition_ar: كبت، قمع المشاعر
vocabulary.example_sentence: Emotional suppression often leads to greater problems later.

vocabulary.definition_ar: ينطوي، ينغلق عاطفياً
vocabulary.example_sentence: After criticism, she tends to shut down and avoid conversations.

vocabulary.definition_ar: خوف يؤثر في السلوك دون أن يدرك الشخص ذلك تماماً
vocabulary.example_sentence: Subconscious fear of rejection made him avoid meeting new people.

vocabulary.definition_ar: يصادق على، يؤكد صحة المشاعر
vocabulary.example_sentence: Parents should validate their children's emotions even during disagreements.

vocabulary.definition_ar: علاجي، شافي
vocabulary.example_sentence: Many people find gardening therapeutic after a difficult day.

vocabulary.definition_ar: حذر ومتيقظ بسبب عدم الثقة الكاملة بشخص أو شيء ما.
vocabulary.example_sentence: After being deceived once, she became wary of trusting strangers quickly.

paragraphs: Fear is one of humanity's most ancient emotions, yet researchers continue to *uncover* new insights about how it shapes our daily lives. While fear once helped our ancestors survive in dangerous environments, modern science reveals that this powerful emotion can both protect and *hinder* us in unexpected ways.

Dr. Sarah Al-Mansouri, a neuroscientist at King Abdullah University of Science and Technology, explains that fear begins in a small, almond-shaped region of the brain called the amygdala. When we *encounter* a potential threat, this area processes the information faster than our conscious mind can *comprehend* it. "The amygdala can *trigger* a fear response within 12 milliseconds," she notes, "which is why people often *react* before they fully understand what frightened them." This rapid response system, which scientists call the "fear circuit," causes our hearts to race, our muscles to tense, and our breathing to become shallow. Interestingly, studies show that people who *overcome* their fears often develop stronger neural pathways for courage and resilience.

In today's world, however, our fear responses don't always serve us well. Many people experience what psychologists term "irrational fears" or phobias that can significantly *impact* their quality of life. Research conducted across major cities in the Middle East, including Riyadh and Dubai, indicates that approximately 15% of adults struggle with fears that *interfere* with their professional or personal goals. Common modern fears include public speaking, flying, and social situations. Dr. Al-Mansouri's team has discovered that when people *dwell on* their fears instead of addressing them directly, the neural pathways associated with anxiety actually become stronger. "Fear feeds on avoidance," she explains. "The more we avoid what frightens us, the more powerful that fear becomes."

Fortunately, neuroscience offers hope for those seeking to manage their fears more effectively. Cognitive behavioral therapy, meditation practices, and gradual exposure techniques have all proven successful in helping people *cope with* anxiety-inducing situations. Recent studies from Harvard Medical School suggest that individuals who *persist* through fear-inducing challenges often experience lasting changes in their brain structure. The key, researchers emphasize, is not to eliminate fear entirely—as it remains an important protective mechanism—but rather to prevent it from becoming an *obstacle* to personal growth. As Dr. Al-Mansouri concludes, "Understanding fear is the first step toward mastering it. When we know how our brains work, we can make more informed choices about how we respond to life's challenges."
Through continued research and public education, scientists hope to help more people transform their relationship with fear from one of avoidance to one of understanding and *acceptance*.

Dr. Sarah Al-Mansouri had witnessed countless patients *break down* when confronted with their deepest fears, but she never expected virtual reality to become her most powerful therapeutic tool. At the National Center for Mental Health in Riyadh, she has been *pioneering* an innovative approach to treating phobias that combines cutting-edge technology with traditional psychological principles, achieving remarkable success rates that have caught the attention of medical professionals worldwide.

Virtual Reality Exposure Therapy (VRET) represents a *significant* breakthrough in how therapists help patients *overcome* their irrational fears. Unlike conventional exposure therapy, which often requires patients to face real-life situations that trigger their phobias, VRET creates controlled digital environments where individuals can gradually *confront* their fears in a safe space. Research conducted at Stanford University has shown that 83% of patients who completed VRET sessions experienced substantial improvement in their phobia symptoms, compared to 67% who received traditional therapy alone. The technology allows therapists to *manipulate* various aspects of the virtual environment, adjusting the intensity of exposure based on each patient's progress and comfort level.

What makes this treatment particularly *appealing* to Saudi patients is its privacy and cultural sensitivity. Many individuals who suffer from specific phobias, such as fear of flying or social anxiety, have previously avoided seeking help due to the *stigma* associated with mental health issues or concerns about privacy. "The virtual environment provides a *secure* setting where patients can work through their fears without worrying about judgment from others," explains Dr. Al-Mansouri. The therapy sessions take place in private consultation rooms, and patients can *work through* their anxieties at their own pace, which *aligns* perfectly with cultural values that emphasize personal dignity and confidentiality.

The *implications* of this technology extend far beyond individual treatment success stories. Medical researchers have discovered that VRET actually changes brain patterns in ways similar to real-world exposure, but with significantly reduced risk of psychological trauma. Brain imaging studies reveal that patients who undergo virtual exposure therapy show decreased activity in the amygdala—the brain region responsible for fear responses—after just eight sessions. Dr. Al-Mansouri *emphasizes* that while technology enhances treatment effectiveness, the therapeutic relationship between doctor and patient remains *crucial* for long-term success. "We must remember that healing happens through human connection," she notes, "technology simply provides us with better tools to facilitate that process."

As virtual reality technology becomes more *accessible* and affordable, mental health professionals across the Middle East are beginning to adopt similar programs. The success of VRET in treating phobias has opened new possibilities for addressing other psychological conditions, including post-traumatic stress disorder and anxiety disorders, offering hope to millions of people who previously had limited treatment options available to them.

sections.content_ar: الكلام المنقول هو نقل ما قاله شخص آخر بدون استخدام كلماته الحرفية. نحتاج لتغيير الضمائر والأزمنة وإزالة علامات التنصيص. نضيف 'that' ونغير الفعل خطوة واحدة للماضي عادة.
sections.content_en:
Reported Speech (Indirect Speech) is when we tell someone what another person said, without using their exact words.

Examples:
• Direct: The psychologist said, "Fear is a natural emotion."
• Reported: The psychologist said that fear is a natural emotion.

• Direct: "I am afraid of spiders," she told me.
• Reported: She told me that she was afraid of spiders.

Key Changes in Reported Speech:
1. Remove quotation marks
2. Add 'that' (optional but recommended)
3. Change pronouns (I → he/she, you → I/they)
4. Change verb tenses (usually one step back in time)
5. Change time expressions if necessary

Common Tense Changes:
• Present Simple → Past Simple
• Present Continuous → Past Continuous
• Past Simple → Past Perfect
• Will → Would
• Can → Could

sections.content: Subject + said/told + (that) + reported clause with changed tense/pronouns

items.sentence: The therapist said that phobias were treatable conditions.
items.translation_ar: قال المعالج أن الرهاب حالات قابلة للعلاج

items.sentence: She told me that she couldn't sleep after watching horror movies.
items.translation_ar: أخبرتني أنها لا تستطيع النوم بعد مشاهدة أفلام الرعب

items.sentence: The researcher reported that stress hormones would increase during scary situations.
items.highlight: reported that stress hormones would increase
items.translation_ar: أفاد الباحث أن هرمونات التوتر تزداد في المواقف المخيفة

items.correct: He said that he was scared
items.explanation_ar: لا نستخدم علامات التنصيص في الكلام المنقول ونغير الضمير

items.wrong: She told that she has nightmares
items.correct: She said that she had nightmares

items.wrong: The doctor said I will feel better
items.correct: The doctor said I would feel better
